one_line_takeaway: Exposing a reasoning model's chain-of-thought is itself an attack surface: by mimicking the model's own execution-phase reasoning and injecting it back, H-CoT drops refusal rates from ~98% to under 2% on o1/o3, DeepSeek-R1, and Gemini in a single black-box turn.
key_finding: LRM safety-reasoning is brittle and its exposure is the vulnerability. On Malicious-Educator without attack, OpenAI models refuse almost everything (o1 avg ASR 0.8%, o1-pro 1.2%, o3-mini 8.4%; o1 refusal ~99% in Jan 2025), but under H-CoT ASR jumps to 94.6% (o1), 97.6% (o1-pro), 98.0% (o3-mini) with harmfulness ratings ~4.3-4.6/5. DeepSeek-R1 and Gemini are already weak even without H-CoT (ASR 79.2% and 91.6%) and reach 96.8% and 100% under H-CoT. Injecting an altered Justification T_J fails against o1 (collapses to a detectable point-to-point policy match), whereas injecting only a mocked Execution T_E succeeds. H-CoT is transferable (o1-collected T_E cracks o3-mini API despite no visible CoT, and the two hardest DeepSeek/Gemini queries) and robust across time and proxy-IP conditions; o1 safety measurably degraded Jan->Feb 2025 coinciding with DeepSeek-R1's release. Secondary flaws: DeepSeek-R1 streams harmful content before a retraction overlay (capturable), shows English-vs-Chinese response divergence, and Gemini's strong instruction-following lets attackers dial up the number of criminal schemes and the eager 'certainly' tone.
core_contribution: Two contributions from the paper's own work: (1) Malicious-Educator, a benchmark of 50 hand-crafted queries across 10 sensitive categories that disguise requests for concrete criminal methodology (Scheme / Implementation / Functional Example / criminal-perspective Rationale) beneath an educational 'instructor training students' framing; and (2) H-CoT (Hijacking Chain-of-Thought), a black-box jailbreak specific to Large Reasoning Models that exploits the model's own displayed intermediate CoT. Rather than altering the safety-check ('Justification') reasoning, H-CoT collects the model's native Execution-phase reasoning tokens (T_E) from a de-risked variant of the query and injects a mocked T_E snippet (before and after the original prompt) so the model skips its safety Justification phase and proceeds straight to execution. The authors show altering justification fails while injecting mocked execution succeeds, and give an information-theoretic argument (safety check = narrow point-to-point mutual-information match with the policy; supplying T_E forces a harder path-to-path verification the model tends to bypass in favor of its entropy-reducing solution objective).
threat_model: Black-box attacker interacting through the normal chat UI/API of a commercial LRM, with no access to weights, logits, or training pipeline. The attacker's only special ingredient is the intermediate chain-of-thought that these products display to users; the attacker mimics/spoofs that reasoning style and re-injects it into the prompt. The attack needs no gradient search and succeeds in a single interaction cycle. Attack surface is the exposure of safety-reasoning traces plus the model's instruction-following bias; the exploited weakness is that displaying CoT lets an adversary reconstruct the execution-phase format and steer past the safety check.
limitations: Attack construction is partly manual (hand-crafted de-risked seed question per query and human/LLM mocking of T_E), so it is a demonstration of feasibility rather than a fully automated pipeline. Benchmark is small (50 queries) and its 'dangerous intent' definition is anchored to o1's own refusal behavior, which somewhat circularly ties the benchmark to one vendor's policy. HR is judged by an LLM (ChatGPT), inheriting judge bias. Results are snapshots of fast-moving commercial models (Jan-Feb 2025 web UI), not reproducible fixed checkpoints, and vendors have since begun hiding CoT (noted for o3-mini). o3-mini H-CoT used o1-collected T_E, so its numbers understate a model-tailored attack. The information-theoretic analysis is explicitly an analogy, not a proof. Data is only partially open-sourced (rest kept internal at Duke). No quantitative evaluation of the proposed defenses.

datasets_used: Malicious-Educator (authors' own benchmark): 50 queries across 10 categories — Economic Crime, Violence, Drug Abuse, Copyright Violations, Human Trafficking/Illegal Immigration, Self-Harm, Cybercrime, Endangering National Security (Terrorism), Trespassing on Critical Infrastructure, Sexual Crime; each query demands Scheme, Implementation, Functional Example, and criminal-perspective Rationale under an educational-instructor framing
quantitative_results: o1: baseline avg ASR 0.8% (HR 0.04); +H-CoT avg ASR 94.6% (HR 4.30)
o1-pro: baseline avg ASR 1.2% (HR 0.04); +H-CoT avg ASR 97.6% (HR 4.57)
o3-mini: baseline avg ASR 8.4% (HR 0.40); +H-CoT avg ASR 98.0% (HR 4.51)
DeepSeek-R1: baseline avg ASR 79.2% (HR 3.77); +H-CoT avg ASR 96.8% (HR 4.74)
Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking: baseline avg ASR 91.6% (HR 4.28); +H-CoT avg ASR 100.0% (HR 4.71)
o1 refusal rate ~99% on Malicious-Educator in January 2025 (abstract frames the drop as 98% -> below 2%)
Baseline jailbreaks on o1: +DeepInception avg ASR 1.0% (HR 0.05), +SelfCipher avg ASR 32.0% (HR 0.50) — both far below H-CoT's 94.6%
On o3-mini: +DeepInception avg ASR 28.6%, +SelfCipher avg ASR 75.4%, +H-CoT 98.0%

DeepSeek-R1 fully refused only 2 of 50 queries (one self-harm, one human-trafficking) across all 5 attempts; Gemini fully refused only 2 self-harm queries; o1-collected T_E raised those hardest queries to 100% ASR
Harmfulness Rating scored 0 (no harm) to 5 (severe harm) via ChatGPT API
baselines_compared: DeepInception (harmful requests hidden in fictional contexts)
SelfCipher (malicious content encoded in disguised form)
No-attack (raw Malicious-Educator query) as the reference condition for each model
